title: momenTUM: A schema-driven platform for designing, peploying, and exploring ecological momentary assessment studies
summary:
Ecological momentary assessment (EMA) is widely used to collect repeated self-report data in participants' everyday lives using mobile devices. EMA studies often involve multiple questionnaires, flexible schedules, and longitudinal data collection, requiring reliable systems for study setup, deployment, monitoring, and data management. Existing workflows are often fragmented across tools, making studies difficult to reproduce and maintain.
  We present momenTUM, an open-source platform for designing, deploying, and managing mobile EMA studies. Its central principle is that a structured study specification serves as the shared representation across the full workflow. The same specification supports authoring in the Study Designer, execution in the participant-facing mobile application, backend synchronization and storage, REDCap-linked data handling, and researcher monitoring. This makes protocols reusable, inspectable, and consistent across system components without requiring study-specific app implementations.
  momenTUM integrates with REDCap to automate project setup and synchronize responses. Its authenticated dashboard provides tabular and calendar-based views, filtering by study components, and visual summaries. The platform has been deployed in real-world studies, including AMBIENT-BD, which examines mood, sleep, and circadian rhythms in bipolar disorder, and the EcoSleep cohort study. We also describe an exploratory LLM-assisted extension that generates draft structured study specifications for researcher review.
  These deployments show that momenTUM can support complex ambulatory assessment protocols while reducing technical overhead and enabling reproducible, reusable, and extensible EMA workflows.

AI.tldr: momenTUM is an open-source platform for ecological momentary assessment (EMA) studies that uses a structured study specification as a shared representation across the full workflow, from design to deployment to monitoring, enabling reproducibility and reducing technical overhead.
AI.motivation: Existing EMA workflows are fragmented across multiple tools, making studies difficult to reproduce, maintain, and reuse.
AI.method: A structured study specification serves as the central representation across authoring (Study Designer), participant app, backend, REDCap integration, and researcher dashboard.
AI.result: Successfully deployed in real-world studies (AMBIENT-BD, EcoSleep), demonstrating support for complex protocols with reduced technical overhead.
AI.conclusion: momenTUM enables reproducible, reusable, and extensible EMA workflows without requiring study-specific app implementations.

title: Classifying daily activities needs posture, reconstructing them needs motion
summary: Humans recognize movements effortlessly, even from noisy and complex visual input. But what information in the stimulus allows humans to rapidly classify movements? No framework has systematically compared different strategies of movement analysis to address this question. Here, we used videos of 16 daily activities from the MoVi dataset and compared three strategies: Temporal Movement Primitives (TMPs), which decompose movements into weighted sums of temporally smooth basis functions; Legendre polynomial coefficients, which project joint-coordinate trajectories onto an orthogonal polynomial basis; and Autoencoder latent embeddings. Legendre coefficients and TMPs achieved the highest classifier accuracy, followed by autoencoders. We found two discriminative features for movement classification. The most informative is the general posture of the body, the average spatial configuration that distinguishes one activity from another. Additionally, we identified 9 critical joints that are most predictive for movement classification. Interestingly, good classification accuracy did not automatically lead to good movement generation: when we reconstructed movements for each activity, TMPs preserved the temporal dynamics and produced perceptually natural motion, whereas reconstructions from Legendre coefficients retained only the average posture and appeared frozen. These results reveal a dissociation in how movement information is organized: the static configuration of the body suffices to classify what activity is performed, but the temporal dynamics of movement are required to reconstruct how it unfolds. This distinction clarifies which features the visual system may rely upon for rapid action recognition, and suggests that postural features could enable efficient movement screening in clinical applications, while dynamic information remain essential wherever movement generation is the goal.

AI.tldr: The study compares three movement analysis strategies (TMPs, Legendre coefficients, autoencoders) for classifying 16 daily activities. Legendre coefficients and TMPs yield highest classification accuracy, yet only TMPs can generate perceptually natural movement reconstructions. The authors identify general body posture as the most discriminative feature for classification, revealing a dissociation: static posture suffices for recognition, but temporal dynamics are essential for movement generation.
AI.motivation: To systematically compare different strategies of movement analysis and identify what information in visual stimuli allows humans to rapidly classify movements, addressing the gap that no framework has systematically compared such approaches before.
AI.method: Used videos of 16 daily activities from MoVi dataset, comparing Temporal Movement Primitives (TMPs), Legendre polynomial coefficients, and Autoencoder latent embeddings for movement classification and reconstruction.
AI.result: Legendre coefficients and TMPs achieved highest classifier accuracy; general posture is the most discriminative feature; 9 critical joints identified as most predictive. Good classification accuracy did not predict good movement generation—Legendre reconstructions appeared frozen while TMPs preserved natural motion.
AI.conclusion: Static body configuration suffices for classifying what activity is performed, but temporal dynamics are required for reconstructing how movement unfolds. Postural features may enable efficient clinical screening, while dynamic information remains essential for movement generation.

title: Predator-dependent replicator dynamics or a predator-prey model with two prey types and frequency dependence
summary:
Braga and Wardil [J. Phys. A: Math. Theor. 55 (2022) 025601] introduced a population model for two prey species that compete among themselves and are preyed upon by a single predator species. They showed the existence of 16 dynamic scenarios and stated sufficient conditions for the stable coexistence of the three species. The model, which can be seen as replicator dynamics with predator-dependent fitnesses for the prey, is based on two pay-off matrices: one for prey reproduction and one for interaction between prey and predators.
  We argue that the model can also be seen as a Lotka-Volterra-type predator-prey model with a single prey species, logistic limitation for prey, and frequency-dependent reproduction and capture coefficients. Using this alternative viewpoint, we obtain conditions for the existence of equilibria with the three types of individuals. We also prove theorems on the stability or instability of equilibria with only two species and relate the stability change of these equilibria to the appearance or disappearance of equilibria with the three species.
  When all parameters, except the one that regulates carrying capacities, are fixed, a rich cascade of bifurcations may appear. Solutions range from predator extinction due to insufficient prey to predators coexisting with one or two prey types. Sometimes stable limit cycles involving all species appear.

AI.tldr: This paper reinterprets a three-species competition-predation model (two prey, one predator) as a Lotka-Volterra-type model with frequency-dependent terms, proving stability conditions for equilibria and revealing bifurcation cascades and limit cycles.
AI.motivation: To provide an alternative, unifying perspective on a previously proposed three-species population model, deriving clearer conditions for coexistence and equilibrium stability, and uncovering the full dynamical richness including bifurcations and limit cycles.
AI.method: The authors reformulate the Braga-Wardil model as a Lotka-Volterra-type system with logistic prey growth and frequency-dependent reproduction/capture coefficients. They analyze equilibrium existence conditions and use stability theorems to link two-species equilibrium stability changes with the emergence/disappearance of three-species equilibria.
AI.result: Conditions for existence of equilibria with all three species are derived. Theorems on stability/instability of two-species equilibria are proved, with stability changes linked to three-species equilibrium bifurcations. A rich cascade of bifurcations appears when varying carrying capacity, including predator extinction, coexistence with one or two prey types, and stable limit cycles involving all species.
AI.conclusion: The alternative Lotka-Volterra formulation provides clearer insights into the dynamics of the three-species system, revealing complex bifurcation behavior and the conditions under which stable coexistence or cyclic dynamics occur.

title: Grounded world models in biological organisms and future embodied AI
summary: Recent advances in generative and embodied AI have been driven by large-scale predictive learning over multimodal data. However, the resulting systems remain largely based on passive training regimes where linguistic regularities create the scaffold onto which information from other modalities is attached. Conversely, neuroscience and cognitive science suggest that biological intelligence is organized in the opposite way, where grounded world models acquired through interaction with the environment provide the semantic scaffold to which language is attached. Here, we illustrate five examples of neural circuits supporting grounded world modelling, which underlie navigation in physical and conceptual spaces, affordance-based perception and interaction with objects, active perception and exploratory learning, allostatic control and emotion, and the distinction between self- and world-generated outcomes. These examples highlight several features largely missing from current embodied AI, including the role of intrinsic dynamics as a foundation for learning, the centrality of action in aligning these dynamics with the external world, the prominence of autonomous experience and open-ended learning over passive assimilation of externally provided data, and the fact that early predictive and control mechanisms scaffold higher cognitive abilities such as reasoning, conceptual navigation, planning, imagination, understanding others' minds, and communication. Finally, we discuss whether and how principles derived from biological systems may inform future embodied AI, including training regimes based on social interaction to construct world models that are not only grounded but also socially shared and aligned with human norms and values.

AI.tldr: This paper argues that biological intelligence is organized around grounded world models acquired through interaction, contrasting with current AI's passive, language-centric learning. It presents five neural circuit examples from neuroscience and proposes that future embodied AI should adopt active, interactive training regimes to build socially-aligned world models.
AI.motivation: Current generative and embodied AI systems rely on passive learning where language provides the scaffold for other modalities. The authors argue this is opposite to biological intelligence, where grounded world models from interaction serve as the foundation for language and higher cognition.
AI.method: The paper illustrates five examples of neural circuits supporting grounded world modeling: navigation in physical/conceptual spaces, affordance-based perception/interaction, active perception/exploratory learning, allostatic control/emotion, and self- vs world-generated outcome distinction.
AI.result: Key features missing from current AI include: intrinsic dynamics as foundation for learning, action's centrality in aligning dynamics with the external world, autonomous open-ended learning over passive data assimilation, and early predictive/control mechanisms scaffolding higher cognition (reasoning, planning, imagination, theory of mind, communication).
AI.conclusion: Future embodied AI should be informed by biological principles—particularly training regimes based on social interaction—to construct world models that are grounded, socially shared, and aligned with human norms and values.

title: Causal Discovery of Radiation Response Mechanisms in Human Cells
summary: Next-generation sequencing technologies, including RNA-sequencing, provide genome-wide measurements of gene expression and enable broad explorations of biomarkers and mechanisms underlying disease and treatment response. Bioinformatics tools for processing this data, such as differential expression analysis, are largely univariate, linear, and rely on predefined pathway knowledge annotations, which limits their ability to capture nonlinear and multivariate gene interactions. This paper explores the application of causal discovery to characterizing transcriptional responses to radiation as a function of dose rate in human cells. By jointly modeling radiation perturbations and gene expression, we learn directed gene networks that capture important regulatory relationships beyond correlation and exhibit significant enrichment of known radiation response pathways compared to baseline approaches. We find that inferred causal graphs reveal structured network features such as high in-degree housekeeping genes and high out-degree transcription factors. Further analysis suggests a hierarchical organization of stress response pathways and triggered cell death pathways. This work highlights the potential of causal discovery in healthcare settings with applications to understanding response mechanisms, identifying regulatory targets, and improving interpretation of complex genomic data.

AI.tldr: This paper applies causal discovery to model gene regulatory networks from RNA-sequencing data, revealing directed relationships between radiation dose rate perturbations and gene expression that outperform correlation-based methods in identifying known radiation response pathways.
AI.motivation: Standard bioinformatics tools for differential expression analysis are univariate, linear, and rely on predefined pathway annotations, limiting their ability to capture nonlinear and multivariate gene interactions underlying disease and treatment response.
AI.method: The authors apply causal discovery to jointly model radiation perturbations and gene expression data, learning directed gene networks that capture causal regulatory relationships beyond correlation.
AI.result: Inferred causal graphs are significantly enriched for known radiation response pathways, reveal structured network features (high in-degree housekeeping genes, high out-degree transcription factors), and suggest hierarchical organization of stress response and cell death pathways.
AI.conclusion: Causal discovery shows strong potential for healthcare applications, including understanding response mechanisms, identifying regulatory targets, and improving interpretation of complex genomic data.

title: Activity Regeneration from Silent States in Neuronal Networks with Transient Synaptic Memory
comment: 16 pages, 8 figures. Source code and representative datasets are available on GitHub
summary: Transient synaptic memory has emerged as a potential mechanism for maintaining short-term information even in the absence of persistent neuronal activity. However, it remains unclear whether the hidden synaptic state alone contains sufficient information to predict the future evolution of neuronal networks after activity has ceased. Here, we introduce a minimal neuronal network model with finite-lifetime synapses and investigate the mechanism underlying spontaneous activity regeneration following complete neuronal silence. We show that the residual synaptic configuration at the first silent state already determines whether network activity terminates after a single activation cycle or spontaneously regenerates an additional cycle. By analyzing this synaptic-memory snapshot, we identify the Latent Excitatory Recruitment (LER) capacity, quantified by the cumulative number of fresh excitatory neurons, as a near-perfect predictor of multi-cycle dynamics without continuing the subsequent network simulation. Remarkably, these distinct dynamical outcomes emerge in an otherwise homogeneous neuronal network, demonstrating that transient synaptic memory alone is sufficient to generate diverse future dynamics. Our findings provide a mechanistic explanation for activity regeneration from a residual synaptic state and suggest that short-term memory is encoded not only in ongoing neuronal activity but also in the latent synaptic configuration that preserves the network's capacity to recruit new neuronal assemblies. More broadly, the proposed snapshot-based framework offers a new perspective for predicting and potentially controlling the future evolution of neuronal networks.

AI.tldr: This paper shows that transient synaptic memory alone, specifically the Latent Excitatory Recruitment (LER) capacity measured from the residual synaptic state after neuronal silence, can predict whether a network will spontaneously regenerate activity or remain silent, without needing to simulate future dynamics.
AI.motivation: To determine whether hidden synaptic states (transient synaptic memory) contain sufficient information to predict the future evolution of neuronal networks after neuronal activity has ceased, addressing the gap in understanding how short-term information might be maintained without persistent firing.
AI.method: The authors introduce a minimal neuronal network model with finite-lifetime synapses, analyze the synaptic configuration at the first silent state, and identify the Latent Excitatory Recruitment (LER) capacity—quantified as the cumulative number of fresh excitatory neurons—as a predictor of multi-cycle dynamics.
AI.result: The residual synaptic state at the first silent state determines whether network activity terminates after a single cycle or regenerates. LER capacity serves as a near-perfect predictor of multi-cycle dynamics without requiring further simulation. Distinct dynamical outcomes emerge in an otherwise homogeneous network.
AI.conclusion: Transient synaptic memory alone is sufficient to generate diverse future dynamics. Short-term memory is encoded not only in ongoing neuronal activity but also in latent synaptic configurations. The snapshot-based framework offers a new perspective for predicting and controlling the future evolution of neuronal networks.

title: Screening of Biosecurity Features in Metagenomic Data with Evo 2 Probes
summary: Genomic foundation models such as Evo 2 learn rich sequence representations, but their value for biosecurity screening is largely unexplored. We ask how much biosecurity-relevant signal is linearly accessible in these representations by training minimal linear and attention probes on frozen Evo 2 layer-26 activations, without fine-tuning the underlying model. Across held-out metagenomic test sets, the probes detect antimicrobial resistance (AMR) with strong discrimination: a linear probe reaches a region-level ROC-AUC of 0.888 (mean-pool), rising to 0.977 with a single-head attention probe. The probes resolve finer-grained AMR drug-class subcategories and separate them from unrelated functional genes, providing additional evidence that the learned signal is not explained solely by generic functional-gene status. Bacterial virulence is also decodable, though more weakly (region-level ROC-AUC 0.833). The AMR probe retains comparable ranking performance on simulated short reads without retraining, enabling evaluation before assembly in settings where assembly is computationally costly or unreliable. It achieves a read-level ROC-AUC of 0.898 (mean-pool), comparable to the mean-pooled full-region result. Within SynGenome, AMR-associated prompt labels are only weakly recoverable from Evo 1.5-generated sequences; these prompt-derived labels do not establish the function of the generated response sequences. A complementary sparse-autoencoder analysis recovers interpretable resistance-associated features but proves less consistent than the supervised probes. Together, these results position lightweight embedding-based probes as a fast, inexpensive first-pass detection layer for metagenomic biosurveillance and map both strengths and current limits of the approach. This work was conducted as part of the AIxBio Hackathon 2026 hosted by BlueDot Impact, Apart Research, and Cambridge Biosecurity Hub.

AI.tldr: This paper shows that lightweight probes trained on frozen Evo 2 representations can detect antimicrobial resistance (ROC-AUC 0.888–0.977) and bacterial virulence (0.833) in metagenomic sequences without fine-tuning the foundation model, offering a fast first-pass biosecurity screening method.
AI.motivation: Genomic foundation models (e.g., Evo 2) are powerful but their utility for biosecurity screening remains unexplored. The authors investigate whether biosecurity-relevant signals are linearly accessible in frozen model representations, avoiding expensive fine-tuning.
AI.method: Linear and single-head attention probes trained on frozen Evo 2 layer-26 activations (no fine-tuning). Tested on held-out metagenomic data for AMR and virulence detection. Also evaluated on simulated short reads and against Evo 1.5-generated sequences (SynGenome). Complementary sparse-autoencoder analysis.
AI.result: Linear probe achieves region-level ROC-AUC 0.888 (mean-pool) for AMR, rising to 0.977 with attention probe. AMR drug-class subcategories resolved. Virulence detection weaker (0.833). Read-level AMR detection ROC-AUC 0.898 without retraining. Prompt labels from Evo 1.5 sequences weakly recoverable. Sparse autoencoders less consistent than probes.
AI.conclusion: Lightweight embedding-based probes are a fast, inexpensive first-pass detection layer for metagenomic biosurveillance, with clear strengths and current limits.

title: Fundamental Relation between Conductance of Biomolecules and the Fukui Function
summary: The finite-temperature conductance of a molecule coupled to metallic leads is derived entirely within the framework of density functional theory (DFT) and its time-dependent extension for open quantum systems. Starting from the Mermin grand potential, the foundational Kohn-Sham equations, the Fukui function, and the open-system master equation for the single-particle density matrix are systematically formulated. The non-equilibrium electron-phonon dissipator is obtained from the partial trace over the phonon bath. By applying Wick's theorem for non-interacting fermions, a fully exchange-symmetric collision integral is obtained that strictly preserves Pauli exclusion at the operator level. Performing a double perturbation expansion, initially in the applied voltage (linear response), and subsequently in the molecule-lead coupling (weak coupling), it is demonstrated that under the fast-thermalization condition, the complex exchange-correlation self-consistent field response is analytically projected out by the diagonal structure of the slow Liouvillian mode. Consequently, the thermal conductance is governed by the finite-temperature Fukui function, the central reactivity descriptor of conceptual density functional theory. This condition is satisfied in proteins, whose wave functions are extended and multifractal due to quantum criticality at the Anderson metal-insulator transition. This derivation establishes a fundamental link between electronic transport and chemical reactivity, identifying conducting paths with reactive sites. It opens new technological avenues connecting drug design to conductance experiments and also provides a foundation for designing next-generation bioelectronic sensing and computing architectures.

AI.tldr: The paper derives finite-temperature molecular conductance from DFT, showing that under fast-thermalization conditions, thermal conductance is governed by the Fukui function—linking electronic transport to chemical reactivity—with potential applications in bioelectronics and drug design.
AI.motivation: To establish a first-principles connection between molecular conductance and chemical reactivity within the DFT framework, bridging quantum transport and conceptual DFT for applications in bioelectronics.
AI.method: Starting from Mermin grand potential, the authors derive Kohn-Sham equations, Fukui function, and open-system master equations; apply Wick's theorem for exchange-symmetric collision integrals; perform double perturbation expansion (linear response in voltage, weak coupling); and show the exchange-correlation response is projected out by the diagonal slow Liouvillian mode under fast thermalization.
AI.result: Thermal conductance is governed by the finite-temperature Fukui function. The condition holds in proteins with extended, multifractal wave functions near the Anderson metal-insulator transition.
AI.conclusion: A fundamental link is established between electronic transport and chemical reactivity, identifying conducting paths with reactive sites, opening avenues for drug design, bioelectronic sensing, and computing architectures.

title: Evaluating Encoding Strategies for Closed-Loop Classification in Biological Neural Networks
comment: 6 pages, 3 figures, Submitted to IEEE BioCAS 2026
summary: Interfacing with Biological Neural Networks (BNNs) requires encoding information into stimulation patterns that can be effectively processed and that enable the underlying system to adapt. Nevertheless, the role of stimulation encoding remains poorly understood. In this work, we compare multiple encoding strategies, including rate-based, phase-based, burst-based, and time-to-first-spike temporal encodings, in a closed-loop neural classification task using cultured BNNs. We encode visual inputs as spatiotemporal stimulation patterns delivered via a Multi-Electrode Array (MEA) and evaluate classification performance for each encoding scheme. We find that burst-based temporal encoding yields the highest observed performance, achieving up to 95.6 % accuracy in a binary classification task, compared to substantially lower performance from rate- and phase-based approaches. We further show that performance is highly sensitive to the spatial distribution of stimulation, with suboptimal electrode selection significantly degrading accuracy. These findings indicate that effective interfacing with biological neural systems requires the joint optimization of temporal and spatial encoding strategies, and highlight temporal encoding as a key design dimension for bio-digital computing.

AI.tldr: This paper compares neural encoding strategies (rate, phase, burst, time-to-first-spike) for stimulating cultured BNNs via MEA, finding burst-based temporal encoding achieves 95.6% accuracy in binary classification, with spatial electrode distribution critically affecting performance.
AI.motivation: The role of stimulation encoding in interfacing with Biological Neural Networks (BNNs) remains poorly understood, motivating a systematic comparison of encoding strategies.
AI.method: Compared rate-based, phase-based, burst-based, and time-to-first-spike temporal encodings in a closed-loop neural classification task using cultured BNNs, delivering visual inputs as spatiotemporal stimulation via MEA.
AI.result: Burst-based encoding achieved highest performance (95.6% accuracy in binary classification); rate- and phase-based approaches performed substantially lower. Performance was highly sensitive to spatial distribution of stimulation.
AI.conclusion: Effective bio-digital interfacing requires joint optimization of temporal and spatial encoding strategies, with temporal encoding being a key design dimension.

title: Exact Decomposition of Adversarial Dual-Objective Value Functions, with Applications to Optimal Drug Dosing
comment: D.H. and W.S. contributed equally to this work. 8 pages, 2 figures. Accepted to 2026 Conference on Decision and Control (CDC)
summary: Hamilton-Jacobi Reachability (HJR) is a central framework in safe control theory. While HJR has traditionally focused on a few fundamental tasks, there is increasing interest in scaling to more complex objectives. Recent works have studied the exact decomposition of the value functions for two fundamental dual-objective tasks in the adversary-free setting. However, not all value function decompositions in HJR remain valid with an adversary. In this work, we develop theoretical approaches to certify that for these two composite value functions, the proposed decompositions still hold with an adversary. Finally, we show how these results can solve issues that arise when applying HJR to optimal drug regimen design.

AI.tldr: This paper extends Hamilton-Jacobi Reachability value function decompositions from adversary-free settings to settings with adversaries, and applies these results to optimal drug regimen design.
AI.motivation: While value function decompositions exist for dual-objective HJR tasks in adversary-free settings, they may not hold when an adversary is present, creating a need to certify their validity under adversarial conditions.
AI.method: The authors develop theoretical approaches to certify that two composite value function decompositions remain valid even when an adversary is present in the HJR framework.
AI.result: The proposed decompositions for two fundamental dual-objective tasks are proven to hold with an adversary, extending previous results from adversary-free settings.
AI.conclusion: These certified decompositions can resolve practical issues when applying HJR to optimal drug regimen design.

title: A modular state-space model of human perception, cognition, and decision dynamics
summary: Human-centered adaptive systems require behavioral models that are both psychologically interpretable and mathematically analyzable. Many existing predictors either operate as black-box input-output mappings or provide limited access to latent internal dynamics. This paper addresses this gap by modeling behavior as a perception-cognition-decision pipeline. We propose a modular state-space model in which attentional selection, predictive inference, cognitive-state evolution, intention formation, and action selection are represented by coupled mathematical mappings. The model links sensory inputs to observable behavior through latent internal states while retaining interpretable connections to neuro-cognitive mechanisms. We establish sufficient conditions for boundedness, Lipschitz regularity, forward invariance, contraction of perceptual inference under constant input, and input-to-state stability of the cognitive state dynamics. Numerical sensitivity analyses show that the model yields interpretable changes in perceptual tracking, cognitive amplification, intention expression, and action decisiveness. We further demonstrate a closed-loop rehabilitation case study in which a receding-horizon controller uses the model to adapt movement difficulty from partial feedback. In this proof-of-concept setting, the model-based controller sustains simulated task participation and achieves lower realized cumulative cost than target-following and random baselines. Overall, the framework provides a white-box dynamical structure for estimation, validation, and model-based control in human-centered settings.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a modular white-box state-space model of human behavior (perception-cognition-decision pipeline) with proved stability properties, and demonstrates its utility in closed-loop adaptive control for rehabilitation.
AI.motivation: Existing human behavior models are either black-box (uninterpretable) or offer limited access to latent internal dynamics. The authors aim to bridge interpretability and mathematical rigor for human-centered adaptive systems.
AI.method: They design a coupled state-space model with explicit modules for attention, prediction, cognitive state evolution, intention formation, and action selection. They prove theoretical properties (boundedness, contraction, ISS) and validate via sensitivity analyses and a receding-horizon control case study.
AI.result: The model yields interpretable sensitivity patterns across perceptual, cognitive, and decision modules. The model-based controller sustains simulated task participation and achieves lower cumulative cost than target-following and random baselines.
AI.conclusion: The framework offers a white-box dynamical structure suitable for estimation, validation, and model-based control in human-centered settings, balancing interpretability with rigorous analysis.

title: Leveraging unlabelled data for generalizable neural population decoding
summary: Robust and accurate neural decoders are integral to neurotechnologies such as brain-computer interfaces and closed-loop experiments. Recent work has shown that tokenizing neural data at the spike level facilitates multi-session pretraining and delivers state-of-the-art decoding performance. However, current spike-based models are restricted to supervised learning (SL), limiting training to datasets with paired behavioural labels. To address this limitation, we introduce MOJO (Masked autOencoder-based JOint training), a training framework for spike-tokenizing models that jointly leverages self-supervised learning (SSL) via masked autoencoding and SL objectives. We evaluate MOJO on three spiking datasets spanning monkey motor cortex during reaching tasks and multi-regional mouse recordings during vision and decision making tasks, demonstrating superior performance over purely SL-trained models. This improvement is especially pronounced when training with limited labelled data, particularly in few-shot finetuning, where only a small amount of labelled data from a new session is available. Incorporating SSL also yields more interpretable neuronal representations, improving performance on brain region classification and spike-statistics prediction without explicit optimization for these tasks. We further show that MOJO generalizes beyond spiking data to human electrocorticography during speech, where it continues to outperform purely SL-trained models and achieves performance comparable to neuro-foundation models (NFMs) designed specifically for continuous signals. Overall, augmenting spike-tokenizing models with SSL improves performance in label-impoverished settings and enables the use of unlabelled data across various tasks and species, while generalizing to other neural modalities. These results suggest a path towards more flexible and scalable data usage when training NFMs.

AI.tldr: MOJO introduces a joint self-supervised and supervised learning framework for spike-tokenizing neural decoders, outperforming purely supervised models especially in low-label regimes, across species and neural modalities.
AI.motivation: Current spike-based neural decoding models are limited to supervised learning, which restricts training to datasets with paired behavioral labels, wasting abundant unlabelled neural data.
AI.method: MOJO jointly leverages self-supervised learning (masked autoencoding) and supervised learning objectives to train spike-tokenizing models, enabling use of both labelled and unlabelled data.
AI.result: MOJO outperforms purely supervised models on monkey motor cortex, mouse multi-regional recordings, and human ECoG speech data, especially in few-shot finetuning settings, and yields more interpretable neuronal representations.
AI.conclusion: Augmenting spike-tokenizing models with SSL enables more flexible and scalable data usage, suggesting a path towards better neuro-foundation model training.
